planting.note: Plant crowns or divisions in early spring as soon as soil can be worked; keep consistently moist until established.

directions: Plant in consistently moist, humus-rich soil in full to partial shade, setting crowns at or just below soil level with sterile fronds pointing outward. The vase-shaped plant spreads by underground stolons to form large colonies over time; divide every 3–4 years to manage spread and rejuvenate growth. Harvest tightly coiled fiddleheads in early spring when they emerge no more than 2–3 inches from the ground and are still tightly coiled; blanch before eating. Avoid dry or compacted soils, as this fern is adapted to stream banks and flood plains.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in a sheltered, shaded spot; tolerates transplanting well.

directions: Set crowns just below soil level in well-drained but moisture-retentive soil amended with leaf mold or compost. This native fern stays evergreen through winter in zones 6 and warmer, making it valuable for year-round ground cover under deciduous trees. Once established it tolerates moderate drought better than most ferns, though it prefers steady moisture through summer. Old fronds flatten to the ground in winter but remain green; remove them in early spring to allow unobstructed emergence of the new season's fronds.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring once new growth is visible; avoid hard frosts after planting as new fronds are tender.

directions: Plant in consistently moist, humus-rich soil where it receives dappled or full shade; its silver, green, and burgundy fronds hold the best color with some morning light but will fade or bleach in afternoon sun. Division every 3 years in early spring keeps clumps vigorous and provides plants for naturalizing a shaded bed. Remove spent fronds in late winter before new fiddleheads emerge in spring. Protect newly planted divisions from slug damage with iron phosphate bait, as young fronds are particularly vulnerable.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in early spring, barely covering the rhizome with soil; water gently until established.

directions: This elegant native fern requires deep to dappled shade and reliably moist, near-neutral to slightly alkaline soil; it is particularly stunning near water features where humidity stays high. Plant the shallow, creeping rhizome horizontally just below the soil surface and avoid root disturbance once established. The distinctive fan-shaped, horizontally tiered fronds on dark wiry stems die back completely in winter; mark the location to avoid accidentally digging the dormant rhizome. Divide overgrown clumps in early spring by gently separating rhizome sections, each with at least one growing tip.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring through early summer; can also be set out in early fall in warm climates.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-enriched soil in partial to full shade; the coppery-bronze new fronds that emerge in spring and again after stress give this fern its common name. Once established in zones 7 and warmer it remains semi-evergreen, holding most of its attractive glossy deep-green fronds through winter. It tolerates heat and moderate drought better than many ferns, making it a strong choice for southern shade gardens. Divide clumps in early spring every 4–5 years; each division should retain a central crown with attached roots.
